items.q: Does an ageing population only create problems for the UK?
items.correct: No - it raises pension and healthcare costs, but older people also work, volunteer, spend in the economy and provide childcare for families.
items.misconception: An ageing population is purely an economic burden with no benefits.
items.random_wrong: An ageing population means the total population must be falling.
items.why: Students often equate 'ageing' with 'dependent'. Evaluation questions expect both challenges and opportunities of demographic change.

items.q: Does the north-south divide mean everywhere in the South is rich and everywhere in the North is poor?
items.correct: No - it describes average regional differences in income, investment and house prices; both the North and the South contain wealthy and deprived areas.
items.misconception: The divide applies uniformly, so all southern places are wealthy and all northern places are deprived.
items.random_wrong: The north-south divide is a physical wall separating the regions.
items.why: Averages hide local variation; London has some of the UK's most deprived boroughs while parts of the North are affluent.

items.q: Has manufacturing completely disappeared from the UK economy?
items.correct: No - manufacturing declined relative to services, but the UK still produces high-value goods in industries such as aerospace, pharmaceuticals and cars.
items.misconception: Deindustrialisation means the UK no longer makes anything.
items.random_wrong: Manufacturing is the largest sector of the UK economy today.
items.why: Deindustrialisation is a relative shift in employment and GDP share, not the total end of industry.

items.q: Which term describes the UK's rising proportion of people aged 65 and over?
items.why: Correct answer: Ageing population. “Youthful population” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The UK economy today is dominated by which sector?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Services. “Heavy manufacturing” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The 'north-south divide' in the UK refers to differences in what?
items.correct: Average wealth, incomes and house prices between regions
items.why: It's Average wealth, incomes and house prices between regions. “Climate zones only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is an example of UK soft power?
items.correct: The global influence of the BBC and UK universities
items.misconception: The size of the Royal Navy
items.why: It's The global influence of the BBC and UK universities. “The size of the Royal Navy” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly what was the UK's total population at the 2021 census (millions)?
items.why: Exact answer: 67 million. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
